content.subtitle: Relative Pronouns
items: who: to refer to people (and animals when we want to give them a personality)
which: to refer to things and concepts (and animals when we don't want to give them a personality)
whom: a formal word for who; as an object; must be used directly after a preposition
that: a more informal word for who, which, when, where, why; only used in defining relative clauses
when: to refer to time; = in/on/at which
where: to refer to place or situation; = in/at which
why: often after the word reason; = the reason for which; only used in defining relative clauses
whose: the possessive of who and which; can also come after a preposition
what: = the thing(s) which; only used in defining relative clauses

content.subtitle: Examples
items: There are a lot of people **who** find modern architecture difficult to appreciate.
This is the blueprint **which** the engineer submitted last month.
That's the consultant with **whom** I collaborated on the project.
I'll never forget the summer **when** I first visited the Alhambra.
Barcelona, **where** Gaudí spent most of his career, is world-famous for its architecture.
And that's (the reason) **why** I decided to study fine arts.
There are several students in my group **whose** portfolios have been shortlisted.
**What** I find extraordinary is how ancient builders aligned their structures with the stars.

content.subtitle: Prepositions in Relative Clauses
content.description: When we use a preposition with a relative pronoun, it is more formal to put the preposition before the pronoun.
examples: This is the journal **to which I referred.** (very formal)
This is the journal **which I referred to.** (less formal)

content.subtitle: Which Referring to a Whole Clause
content.description: Which can refer to the whole preceding clause, rather than just the preceding noun.
examples: She announced that she was leaving the company, **which really surprised everyone.** (= the announcement surprised us)

content.subtitle: Defining Relative Clauses
content.description: These tell us which one of a group of things/people we are talking about. The sentence doesn't usually make complete sense if we remove the relative clause.
items: We can use **that** instead of who/which/etc. This is more informal.
We don't use a comma or commas.
We can omit the relative pronoun if it is the object.
When, where and why can be omitted.
We cannot put a number or a determiner such as some, none, much and many before of which or of whom.
examples: That's the professor **who gave the keynote lecture.**
That's the professor **that gave the keynote lecture.**
I'll never forget **the afternoon I passed my driving test.** (when omitted)

content.subtitle: Non-Defining Relative Clauses
content.description: These simply give us more information about someone/something. The sentence makes complete sense if we remove the relative clause.
items: We cannot use **that** instead of who/which/etc.
We must use a comma or commas.
We cannot omit the relative pronoun.
We do not use why.
We cannot omit where and when.
We can put a number or a determiner such as some, none, much and many before of which or of whom.
examples: Professor Clarke, **who has been lecturing here for over twenty years**, is a highly respected researcher.
Cambridge, **where she completed her doctorate**, is renowned for its academic excellence.
I enrolled in three seminars, **some of which** were genuinely eye-opening.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: To replace a relative clause
With prepositions and conjunctions
To explain the reason for something
To talk about actions happening at the same time
To talk about actions happening in sequence
As an alternative passive form
As an alternative conditional form

content.subtitle: Examples
items: She was the technician **leading** the research at the time. (who was leading)
The manuscript **discovered in the attic** was over four hundred years old. (which had been discovered)
After **submitting her essay**, she went straight out for a walk.
After **having submitted her essay**, she went straight out for a walk.
**Being allergic to shellfish**, Marcus was very cautious at the seafood buffet.
**Having visited Florence several times before**, Carla felt completely at ease navigating the city.
**Waiting** at the airport gate, I could hear announcements echoing through the terminal.
**Having spotted** a vacancy at the museum, she applied straight away.
**Given** a map of the city, we found the gallery without any trouble. (when/because we were given a map)
**Given** the opportunity, I'd love to spend a year in Japan. (if I were given the opportunity)

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: When the participle clause doesn't have its own subject, the clause and the rest of the sentence must both refer to the same subject.
examples: ✓ **Strolling** through the gallery, I came across a fascinating exhibit. (= I was strolling)
✗ **Strolling** through the gallery, the exhibit caught my eye. (= the exhibit wasn't strolling)

content.subtitle: Uses
items: To start a sentence
examples: **To become** a world-class musician requires years of dedicated practice. (= It requires years of dedicated practice to become a world-class musician.)
My role was **to prepare** the agenda for the annual conference.

content.subtitle: Conjunctions and Phrases
items: although / though / even though
in spite of / despite (+ noun or -ing)
while / whereas
however
other phrases and structures: Try as he might, However hard..., Hard though/as..., Much as...

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Even though she had applied for the scholarship, Clara did not receive an offer.
Despite applying for the scholarship, Clara did not receive an offer.
While solar panels are highly effective in sunny climates, they generate far less energy in overcast regions.
Titanium is an exceptionally strong material. However, it is costly to process.
Try as she might, she simply could not master the art of calligraphy.
However hard she tried, she simply could not master the art of calligraphy.
Much as she tried, she simply could not master the art of calligraphy.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: With in spite of / despite + -ing, both parts of the sentence must refer to the same subject.
items: Even if is used to emphasise that it doesn't matter if something happens or is true, another situation remains the same.
Even if suggests that something may or may not happen, whereas even though suggests that the action actually takes place.
examples: ✓ Even if they found a solution to the energy crisis tomorrow, it would take many years to implement.
✓ Even if she tried to reach the summit, she couldn't. (= She probably hasn't tried yet.)
✓ Even though she tried to reach the summit, she couldn't. (= She tried and was unsuccessful.)

sections.title: B: Replacing with 'That'
sections.instructions: Read the sentences and click the marked relative pronoun only when it can be replaced by that. If it cannot, select OK.

items.sentence: Natasha, [whose] paintings you admired at the gallery last month, is exhibiting again in the spring.
I've never really understood the reason [why] he decided to resign from such a well-paid position.
The coastal town [which] Marcus grew up in is only a short drive from here.
Have you heard from that composer [whose] latest symphony is being premiered next month?
It was back in the early 1990s, when the internet was still a novelty, that hardly anyone [whom] I knew owned a personal computer.
The council has just announced that the old cinema, [where] we all used to go on Friday evenings, will be converted into luxury apartments.
Rio de Janeiro is a city [which] I've always dreamed of visiting.
Do you remember the afternoon [when] we got completely lost on the way to the festival?
The horses [which] are grazing in the far field belong to the local riding school.
The department head to [whom] you should address your application is currently on sabbatical.

sections.title: C:
sections.instructions: Read the sentences and click the marked relative pronoun only when it can be omitted. If it cannot, select OK.

items.sentence: The coastal town [which] Marcus grew up in is only a short drive from here.
It was back in the early 1990s, when the internet was still a novelty, that hardly anyone [whom] I knew owned a personal computer.
Rio de Janeiro is a city [which] I've always dreamed of visiting.
Do you remember the afternoon [when] we got completely lost on the way to the festival?
I've never really understood the reason [why] he decided to resign from such a well-paid position.
Felix was the only candidate [whom] the entire panel agreed was truly outstanding.
Natasha, [whose] paintings you admired at the gallery last month, is exhibiting again in the spring.
The department head to [whom] you should address your application is currently on sabbatical.

sections.title: D: Error Correction
sections.instructions: Read the full text and click the comma pills where a comma is needed.
sections.passage: My sister who lives in Madrid [[,]] is visiting next week [[,]] and my brother who works in Lisbon is coming too [[,]] if he can leave early. The report that you sent yesterday [[,]] was very clear [[,]] and we can submit it this afternoon.

sections.title: E: Commas in Relative Clauses
sections.instructions: Add commas to the sentences where necessary.

items.sentence: We visited the museum that you had recommended so highly.
The documentary which was produced by Channel 4 won three international awards.
items.answer: The documentary, which was produced by Channel 4, won three international awards.

items.sentence: The person who I admire most in the world is my grandmother.
I ran into Owen at the conference which was an unexpected coincidence.
items.answer: I ran into Owen at the conference, which was an unexpected coincidence.

items.sentence: Is that the report which you were discussing with the team earlier?
The head of department who taught here for thirty years has just announced her retirement.
items.answer: The head of department, who taught here for thirty years, has just announced her retirement.

items.sentence: Have you found a candidate who meets all the requirements without needing additional training?
Edinburgh University where I spent four unforgettable years consistently ranks among the top universities in the country.
items.answer: Edinburgh University, where I spent four unforgettable years, consistently ranks among the top universities in the country.

sections.title: F: Relative Clauses with Prepositions
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Do you really believe the decade ...... which you were born shapes your outlook on life?
They signed the peace agreement, the reason ...... which historians are still debating today.
There are six chairs remaining, three ...... which we should set aside for the guest speakers.
Is the date ...... which the ceremony takes place a public holiday?
Rafael solved the problem brilliantly, ...... which the whole team was enormously grateful.
There were 25 applicants for the research grant, some ...... whom had already published extensively.
The policy needs to be reformed, ...... which I mean the entire approval process needs overhauling.
...... whom should we address the formal complaint?
I reviewed several potential venues for the conference, none ...... which were entirely suitable.
She lectured at the institute from 1987 to 1994, ...... which time the discipline underwent a radical transformation.
The valley ...... which most of the vineyards are located lies further to the south.
The director produced over thirty documentaries, several ...... which have gone on to win major awards.

sections.title: G: Rewrite with Relative Clauses
sections.instructions: Rewrite as one sentence using a relative clause.

items.sentence: That building is a Grade II listed structure. An architect restored it in 1902.
items.answer: That building, which an architect restored in 1902, is a Grade II listed structure.

items.sentence: Marco and Sofia are opening a new gallery next month. They are childhood friends.
items.answer: Marco and Sofia, who are childhood friends, are opening a new gallery next month.

items.sentence: On the Tuesday, we spent most of the afternoon exploring the old quarter. On the Tuesday, the guided tour was cancelled.
items.answer: On the Tuesday, when the guided tour was cancelled, we spent most of the afternoon exploring the old quarter.

items.sentence: Ruth used to head the marketing division. You bumped into her at the conference last Thursday.
items.answer: Ruth, whom you bumped into at the conference last Thursday, used to head the marketing division.

items.sentence: Let's try Sakura's. They do an excellent vegetarian menu there.
items.answer: Let's try Sakura's, where they do an excellent vegetarian menu.

items.sentence: This map was once used by early explorers. It was donated to us by a local historian.
items.answer: This map, which was donated to us by a local historian, was once used by early explorers.

items.sentence: Why did Laura decide to take on such a demanding project? She's completely overwhelmed.
items.answer: Why did Laura, who is completely overwhelmed, decide to take on such a demanding project?

items.sentence: Patrick is now collaborating with my colleague. His sister is married to my brother.
items.answer: Patrick, whose sister is married to my brother, is now collaborating with my colleague.

sections.title: H: Participle Clauses
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: After **finishing / having finished** that enormous meal, it's hardly surprising you can't move.
**Having been briefed / Briefing** thoroughly on the agenda beforehand, I felt well-prepared for the meeting.
**Given / Giving** the chance, would you apply for a position overseas?
Miriam was the only delegate **keeping / who was keeping** detailed notes throughout the session.
**Having come across / Coming across** an interesting vacancy online, she decided to apply immediately.
The cyclist **injured / having injured** in the race was taken to a nearby hospital.
**Not having / Not to have** a valid ticket, the passenger was asked to leave the train.
After **submitted / having submitted** the application, I heard nothing back for three weeks.
**Informed / Having been informed** of the delay, passengers began searching for alternative routes.
**Glancing / Having glanced** through the window, Sophie noticed a light still on in the office.
**To land / Landing** a place at a top conservatoire is the ambition of many young musicians.
We were reminded that we were **to return / returning** the signed forms by the following Friday.

sections.title: I: Participle Clause Forms
sections.instructions: Write the verb in brackets in the correct form in each hint pill.

items.sentence: I'd love to work on an international project, (1) ...... (give) the opportunity.
After (2) ...... (get) such critical feedback on the first draft, she rewrote the entire chapter.
The three candidates (3) ...... (select) for the final round have not yet been publicly announced.
We were only offered a refund after (4) ...... (complain) directly to the store manager.
(5) ...... (visit) the exhibition already, Priya was eager to hear her friend's first impressions.
(6) ...... (cycle) along the coastal path, the tourists were thrilled to spot a pod of dolphins.
(7) ...... (send) in her resignation, she felt an immediate sense of relief.
Is the Ritz the only hotel in the city (8) ...... (host) the symposium this year?
(9) ...... (wish) to cause offence, Claire chose her words very carefully.
It's the project manager's role (10) ...... (coordinate) all the different departments throughout the process.
(11) ...... (injure) in the opening set, the tennis player struggled to maintain her usual pace.
(12) ...... (collect) vintage maps is a surprisingly popular hobby!

sections.title: J: Concession Clauses
sections.instructions: Choose the correct option (A, B, C or D) for each sentence.

items.sentence: ...... experienced coaches and seasoned athletes predicted that the solo crossing would end in failure, the young adventurer set off alone across the Pacific.
She battled fierce storms and repeated equipment failures, ...... , and nearly abandoned the attempt on several occasions.
Determined ...... she was, there were moments when giving up felt like the only sensible course of action.
...... battling severe exhaustion and a painful injury, she maintained a meticulous logbook every single day at sea.
Her story doesn't end with the crossing, ...... .
...... her having spent over two hundred days at sea in complete isolation, she displayed no signs of lasting psychological damage.
She noted that ...... some days were filled with despair, others brought unexpected moments of profound calm.
...... the psychologist found her account difficult to believe at first, a subsequent investigation confirmed her observations.
...... as they tried, researchers were unable to fully explain why her psychological resilience proved so exceptional.
Even ...... one believes that human beings are fundamentally resilient, rowing across an ocean alone for two hundred days pushes that conviction to its very limits.

sections.title: K: Sentence Transformation
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence: Despite having searched the entire house, Oliver never found his lucky coin. **he**
items.answer: Even **though he had searched** the entire house, Oliver never found his lucky coin.

items.sentence: The renovation won't be finished before the opening whether the builders start right away or not. **even**
items.answer: The renovation won't be finished before the opening **even if the builders start** right away.

items.sentence: The technicians are on strike but the service is still running. **spite**
items.answer: The service is still running **in spite of the fact that** the technicians are on strike.

items.sentence: Although nobody sent us directions, we still managed to find the venue. **given**
items.answer: We still managed to find the venue in **spite of not being given** any directions.

items.sentence: Tom couldn't solve the puzzle despite really trying his hardest. **might**
items.answer: Try **as he might**, Tom couldn't solve the puzzle.

items.sentence: Although Rachel sang beautifully, she still wasn't selected for the lead role. **might**
items.answer: However **beautifully she might sing**, Rachel still wasn't selected for the lead role.

items.sentence: I couldn't convince Maria in spite of my pleading with her to stay. **as**
items.answer: Much **as I pleaded with her**, I couldn't convince Maria to stay.

items.sentence: Despite our being exhausted, we didn't consider for a moment that we wouldn't reach the summit. **though**
items.answer: Exhausted **though we were**, we didn't consider for a moment that we wouldn't reach the summit.

sections.title: M: Cloze Text
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Most major museums will hold at least one 'palimpsest', (1) ...... is the technical term for a manuscript that has been partially erased or overwritten, in their collection.
A curator (2) ...... collection, let's say, includes such a document will typically arrange for a full scientific analysis.
(3) ...... completed the initial examination, the conservator will decide on the most appropriate method of treatment.
For a manuscript (4) ...... chemical intervention is required, (5) ...... may involve exposing the material to a series of reactive agents under carefully controlled conditions.
Conservators (6) ...... are dealing with more extensive damage may recommend 'consolidation treatment', (7) ...... which a specialist resin is applied layer by layer to stabilise the surface.
(8) ...... , for items with only minor surface damage, a cleaning solution – often (9) ...... from natural plant compounds – is all that is needed.
(10) ...... restoring a complex medieval fresco can take several years of painstaking effort, treating a simple watercolour may take no more than a few weeks.
Their protective coating (11) ...... away, the artefacts will almost certainly require storage in a climate-controlled environment (12) ...... maintains stable temperature and humidity levels.
(13) ...... remarkable advances in imaging technology, (14) ...... decipher a badly damaged inscription is still an enormous challenge.
Even (15) ...... every step of the treatment has been followed correctly, there is no guarantee that the original colours or text will ever be fully restored.
